skill.name: multi-agent-patterns
skill.description: Master orchestrator, peer-to-peer, and hierarchical multi-agent architectures
skill.summary: Master orchestrator, peer-to-peer, and hierarchical multi-agent architectures
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Security evaluation confirms this is a documentation skill about multi-agent architecture patterns. Static findings flagged external_commands, network, and cryptographic patterns but all are FALSE POSITIVES - the scanner misidentified markdown code blocks as shell commands, documentation URLs as HTTP requests, and coincidental keywords as cryptographic usage. The skill contains no executable code, no actual network calls, and no security vulnerabilities.

content.value_statement: Single-agent systems face context limits that constrain complex task handling. Multi-agent architectures distribute work across specialized agents with separate context windows, enabling parallel reasoning and coordination beyond single-agent capabilities.
actual_capabilities: Implement supervisor/orchestrator patterns for centralized task coordination
Design peer-to-peer swarm architectures for flexible agent handoffs
Create hierarchical agent structures with strategic, planning, and execution layers
Apply context isolation techniques to prevent context pollution across agents
Implement consensus mechanisms including weighted voting and debate protocols
Identify and mitigate failure modes such as supervisor bottlenecks and divergence
limitations: This skill provides architectural guidance, not executable code implementations
Token costs increase significantly (approximately 15x baseline) compared to single-agent approaches
Framework-specific implementations (LangGraph, AutoGen, CrewAI) require separate skill knowledge
Does not cover real-time agent communication or streaming patterns

use_cases.title: Research Task Automation
use_cases.description: Coordinate multiple specialized agents (researcher, analyzer, fact-checker, writer) to perform comprehensive research tasks in parallel, with a supervisor aggregating results.
use_cases.target_user: Developers building research assistants and automated analysis pipelines

use_cases.title: Enterprise Workflow Systems
use_cases.description: Deploy hierarchical agent structures that mirror organizational hierarchies, with strategic, planning, and execution layers handling different abstraction levels.
use_cases.target_user: Enterprise architects designing complex workflow automation systems

use_cases.title: Customer Service Routing
use_cases.description: Implement peer-to-peer agent patterns that dynamically handoff customer requests to specialized agents (billing, technical, sales) based on request type.
use_cases.target_user: Teams building intelligent customer support systems

prompt_templates.title: Basic Supervisor Pattern
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a supervisor agent system that coordinates three specialist agents for [TASK]. Include how the supervisor decomposes the task, routes to appropriate specialists, and aggregates results.
prompt_templates.scenario: When you need centralized control with clear task decomposition

prompt_templates.title: Peer-to-Peer Handoff Design
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a peer-to-peer agent architecture for [USE_CASE] where agents can dynamically handoff to each other. Define the handoff protocols and state passing mechanisms.
prompt_templates.scenario: When flexible exploration and emergent problem-solving are needed

prompt_templates.title: Hierarchical Architecture
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a three-layer hierarchical agent system for [DOMAIN]: strategy layer for goal definition, planning layer for task decomposition, and execution layer for atomic tasks.
prompt_templates.scenario: When building large-scale systems with clear abstraction boundaries

prompt_templates.title: Consensus Mechanism Implementation
prompt_templates.prompt: Implement a consensus mechanism for [SCENARIO] that uses weighted voting by agent confidence scores. Include debate protocols for adversarial critique and trigger-based intervention for sycophancy detection.
prompt_templates.scenario: When multiple agents must agree on complex reasoning tasks

output_examples.input: Design a research team multi-agent system
output_examples.output: A supervisor coordinates four specialists: Researcher (web search, document retrieval), Analyzer (data analysis, statistics), Fact-checker (verification, validation), and Writer (report generation). The supervisor decomposes research queries, routes to appropriate agents, and aggregates findings. Use a forward_message tool to allow direct user responses when agents produce final output, avoiding the telephone game problem where supervisors paraphrase sub-agent responses incorrectly.

output_examples.input: When should I use peer-to-peer vs hierarchical patterns?
output_examples.output: Use peer-to-peer/swarm when: tasks require flexible exploration, rigid planning is counterproductive, or requirements emerge dynamically. Advantages: no single point of failure, scales for breadth-first exploration, enables emergent behaviors. Use hierarchical when: large-scale projects with clear structure, enterprise workflows with management layers, or tasks need both high-level planning and detailed execution. Advantages: mirrors organizational structures, clear separation of concerns.

best_practices: Design for context isolation as the primary benefit - sub-agents should have clean, focused contexts rather than carrying accumulated history
Choose architecture pattern based on coordination needs, not organizational metaphors - the supervisor pattern provides control, peer-to-peer provides flexibility, hierarchical provides abstraction
Implement explicit handoff protocols with state passing to prevent context leakage between agents
anti_patterns: Creating sub-agents that mimic organizational roles (CEO, manager, worker) rather than focusing on context partitioning - this anthropomorphizes agents without functional benefit
Allowing supervisor to paraphrase sub-agent responses (the telephone game problem) which loses fidelity in multi-agent systems
Using simple majority voting without weighting by confidence or expertise - weak model hallucinations get equal weight to strong model reasoning

faq.question: What is the main benefit of multi-agent architectures?
faq.answer: The primary benefit is context isolation. Each sub-agent operates in a clean context window focused on its subtask without carrying accumulated context from other tasks. This addresses the context bottleneck that limits single-agent systems.

faq.question: How much more expensive are multi-agent systems compared to single-agent?
faq.answer: Multi-agent systems consume approximately 15x more tokens than single-agent baselines due to coordination overhead, parallel context windows, and result aggregation. However, they enable capabilities beyond single-agent limits.

faq.question: Which architecture pattern should I choose?
faq.answer: Choose supervisor/orchestrator for centralized control and clear task decomposition. Choose peer-to-peer/swarm for flexible exploration and emergent requirements. Choose hierarchical for large-scale systems with clear abstraction layers.

faq.question: How do I prevent supervisor bottlenecks?
faq.answer: Implement output schema constraints so workers return only distilled summaries. Use checkpointing to persist supervisor state without carrying full history. Consider direct pass-through mechanisms allowing sub-agents to respond to users when appropriate.

faq.question: What consensus mechanisms work for multi-agent systems?
faq.answer: Weighted voting (weight by confidence or expertise), debate protocols (adversarial critique over multiple rounds), and trigger-based intervention (detect stalling or sycophancy markers). Avoid simple majority voting which treats weak model hallucinations equally.

faq.question: What frameworks implement these patterns?
faq.answer: LangGraph uses graph-based state machines with explicit nodes and edges. AutoGen uses conversational/event-driven patterns with GroupChat. CrewAI uses role-based process flows with hierarchical crew structures.
